summary: Epic second set with monster Comes A Time jam and tight The Other One
blurb: The show shines in the second set, delivering a sprawling Comes A Time jam, a fiery The Other One, and a powerful Eyes outro. Jerry’s flanged guitar tone soars while Keith anchors the jams with a heavy keyboard presence. The first set is uneven but still offers solid moments like Sugaree.
review: July 17, 1976 at San Francisco’s Orpheum Theatre was a night of contrasts that ultimately tipped in favor of an unforgettable second set. While the opening half drifted – a sluggish start highlighted by a mixed‑up mix on “Sugaree” and occasional vocal dropouts – the band turned it on its head after “Samson & Delilah.” A soaring, flanger‑laced solo from Jerry on “Comes A Time” launched a 16‑minute jam that cascaded into an incendiary “The Other One,” followed by a seamless flow into “Eyes Of The World” and back into another monster “Other One.” Keith’s keyboard outro on “Eyes” was described as “stronger than dirt,” and the dual drummers kept the energy loose and driving. Across all sources, the soundboard capture (gd76-07-17.sbd.unknown.243.sbeok.shnf) is praised for its solid clarity despite a few mix quirks, making it the definitive listening source for this legendary second‑set marathon.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four out of five recordings are flagged as positive and one mixed; reviewers consistently praise the second‑set jams (Comes A Time, The Other One, Eyes) and Jerry’s guitar work while noting only minor mix issues. No recurring negative comments about performance were found, just a single mixed note on matrix echo, so a strong positive adjustment is warranted.

best_recording.reason: Soundboard source with generally clear, well‑balanced audio; captures the monster second set while keeping mix quirks to a minimum.

key_highlights: Monster second set featuring an epic Comes A Time jam and ferocious The Other One
Powerful Eyes outro with Keith’s heavy keyboard presence
Jerry’s flanger‑laden tone and soaring solos dominate the jams
Soundboard recording offers the clearest capture despite occasional mix hiccups

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers soaring, flanger‑rich solos on Comes A Time and The Other One; overall guitar work is praised as powerful and emotional despite occasional vocal dropouts.
band_performance.Phil: Locks in tight bass lines especially during Eyes Of The World; a memorable mic moment (“If you people would shut the f**k up…”) adds character, though his vocals suffer early mix loss.
band_performance.Bob: Quirky guitar work draws mixed reactions but when present fuels the jams with flexibility and energy; occasional mix disappearance noted in early set.
band_performance.Brent: Keith’s keyboards drive the second‑set jams, delivering a “stronger than dirt” outro on Eyes and a heavy presence that some feel is almost too much, yet overall essential to the flow.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums are praised for loose, cowboy‑style energy on New Minglewood Blues and tight support throughout the extended second‑set improvisations.
band_performance.Billy: Shares the rhythmic foundation with Mickey, contributing solid, driving beats that propel the marathon jams without complaint.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
